title: True or False? The Computer Decides!

setup.context: A theme park ride height checker

root.narration: You are programming the height checker for a roller coaster! The rule is: you must be at least 48 inches tall to ride. A kid walks up who is 52 inches tall. The computer checks: 'Is this person at least 48 inches tall?' What does the computer decide?

choices.text: TRUE! 52 is more than 48, so the answer is true. Let them ride!
MAYBE. It depends on other things too.

outcome.headline: TRUE! They Can Ride!
outcome.explanation: Computers answer questions with TRUE or FALSE -- nothing in between! 'Is 52 at least 48?' The answer is TRUE because 52 is bigger than 48. These true/false answers are called BOOLEANS (named after a mathematician named George Boole). Every IF/THEN decision in code uses a boolean: if the answer is TRUE, do one thing. If FALSE, do something else.
outcome.whatToRemember: Computers answer questions with TRUE or FALSE. These yes-or-no answers are called booleans, and they power every decision in code.

keyTerms.definition: A value that can only be TRUE or FALSE. Computers use booleans to make decisions.

outcome.headline: Computers Do Not Do Maybe!
outcome.explanation: In real life, things can be 'maybe' or 'it depends.' But computers only understand TRUE or FALSE! There is no maybe. The question is simple: is 52 at least 48? YES (true) or NO (false). Since 52 IS at least 48, the answer is TRUE.
outcome.whatToRemember: Computers do not have a 'maybe.' Every question gets a TRUE or FALSE answer. That is how they make clear decisions!

outcome.headline: Check Again! 52 is More Than 48!
outcome.explanation: The rule says 'at least 48 inches.' The kid is 52 inches. 52 is MORE than 48, so the answer is TRUE -- they CAN ride! If the kid were 45 inches, THEN the answer would be FALSE. Always compare the numbers carefully when checking true/false.
outcome.whatToRemember: Read the question carefully and compare the numbers. 'At least 48' means 48 or more. 52 is more than 48, so it is TRUE.
